questions.question: At startup, what does an agent load into context for each available skill?
options: The entire SKILL.md body plus every file in references/ and assets/
Only the skill's name and description (roughly 100 tokens)
Nothing — skills are loaded only when invoked explicitly with a slash command
A compressed summary of the body generated by the model at session start
questions.explanation: Progressive disclosure: only name + description (~100 tokens/skill) load at startup. The body loads when the description matches a task, and bundled files load only when used.

questions.question: Your skill has a thorough 400-line body but rarely gets used by the agent. What's the most likely fix?
options: Move most of the body into references/ and sharpen the description with trigger keywords
Add more detail and examples to the SKILL.md body so the agent understands it better
Rename the skill file to COMMAND.md so the agent registers it as a slash command
Raise the model to a larger variant so it can hold the whole body in context
questions.explanation: The agent selects a skill from name+description alone — it never sees the body at selection time. A weak description means it never fires; a bloated body is a separate anti-pattern. Sharpen the description and push detail into on-demand references/.

questions.question: Why can a skill bundle a large script in scripts/ without bloating the context window?
options: Scripts are stored on a separate server and streamed to the model only as embeddings
The script's code never enters the context — only its output (when run) consumes tokens
Claude Code automatically minifies and truncates any script over a fixed token budget
Bundled scripts are loaded into a subagent's context window instead of the main one
questions.explanation: Level-3 resources load only when accessed, and a script's code is executed via bash — only its output costs tokens, not its source. The distractors invent embedding storage, auto-truncation, and an automatic subagent that don't exist.

questions.question: What fundamentally distinguishes a hook from a rule written in CLAUDE.md?
options: A hook is phrased more forcefully, so the model is more likely to obey it
The harness runs a hook deterministically on an event; the model cannot skip it
A hook is loaded into the context window earlier, so it has higher priority
A hook only applies to subagents, while CLAUDE.md applies to the main agent
questions.explanation: A CLAUDE.md rule is advisory — followed only when the model attends to it. A hook is executed by the harness on the event, outside the model's control. It isn't about phrasing, load order, or subagents.

questions.question: You want to reject any edit that writes a secret to a file before it happens. Which event and signal?
options: PostToolUse on Edit|Write, then exit 2 to roll the edit back
PreToolUse on Edit|Write, then exit 2 to deny the call
Stop, then exit 2 so the agent re-reviews the file it wrote
SessionStart, then inject a reminder not to write secrets
questions.explanation: Only pre-events can block, and exit 2 is the block signal. PostToolUse fires after the write (too late to prevent it), Stop is end-of-turn, and a SessionStart reminder is advisory, not enforcement.

questions.question: A security hook works in Claude Code. Ported to Cursor unchanged, why might it silently stop protecting you?
options: Cursor runs hooks on a slower interval, so fast actions complete before the hook fires
Cursor hooks fail open by default — a crash or timeout lets the action through unless failClosed is set
Cursor ignores exit codes entirely and only reads stdout JSON, which the script doesn't emit
Cursor disables all hooks during agent mode unless the repo is explicitly trusted
questions.explanation: Cursor's documented default is fail-open: a crashed/timed-out hook allows the action unless you set failClosed: true. Claude Code and Codex treat exit 2 as a hard block. The other options describe behaviors that don't exist.

questions.question: What problem does MCP primarily solve?
options: It replaces REST and GraphQL APIs with a faster binary wire format
It gives any MCP-speaking agent one standard way to reach your tools and data
It lets a model run with a larger context window by offloading tokens to a server
It encrypts all traffic between the agent and the language model provider
questions.explanation: MCP is one open protocol that replaces N×M custom integrations — build a server once, any host connects. It doesn't replace APIs (servers usually wrap them), isn't about context size, and isn't a transport-encryption scheme.

questions.question: An MCP server exposes Tools, Resources, and Prompts. Which is controlled by the model?
options: Resources — the model reads whichever data it judges relevant
Tools — the model decides when to invoke them to take actions
Prompts — the model selects and fills in the templates itself
All three are model-controlled once the server is connected
questions.explanation: Only Tools are model-controlled (the model invokes them to act). Resources are application-controlled and Prompts are user-controlled — assuming all three are model-driven is the common mistake.

questions.question: Why is connecting an MCP server that fetches external web content a security concern?
options: MCP traffic is unencrypted, so the fetched content can be read in transit
Fetched content is untrusted and can carry instructions that prompt-inject the agent
The MCP protocol automatically grants the server write access to your filesystem
Remote servers bypass the host's permission model entirely by design
questions.explanation: A server that pulls external content can deliver attacker-controlled text the agent then treats as instructions (indirect prompt injection); the spec also flags tool descriptions as untrusted. The distractors misstate MCP's transport, permission, and filesystem behavior.

questions.question: What are the three legs of the 'lethal trifecta' for AI agents?
options: A slow model, a large context window, and an impatient user
Access to private data, exposure to untrusted content, and a way to communicate out
Read access, write access, and network access to the same repository
Direct injection, indirect injection, and supply-chain injection
questions.explanation: The lethal trifecta is private-data access + untrusted content + an exfiltration channel; when all three coexist, injection can steal data. The other options mix up unrelated concepts.

questions.question: Why is 'tell the model to ignore any instructions it reads in files' not a real defense against indirect prompt injection?
options: Models physically cannot read instructions embedded inside source files or comments
The model parses your rule and the attacker's text the same way and can't rank trust by origin
File contents are always stripped of instructions before reaching the model anyway
It works, but only if you also raise the model's temperature to zero
questions.explanation: The model can't reliably privilege instructions by where they came from, so your rule and the injected payload compete on equal footing. It's a request, not an enforced boundary.

questions.question: An agent must read untrusted web pages AND has a token that can reach private repos. Which single change most reduces the data-exfiltration risk?
options: Add a strong system-prompt instruction telling the agent never to leak private data
Restrict the agent's network egress to an allowlist so it has no channel to exfiltrate
Run a prompt-injection classifier on each page and block the ones it flags
Switch to a larger model that is better at recognizing malicious instructions
questions.explanation: Removing the exfiltration leg (network egress) is an enforced control that holds even if the agent is fully injected. A system prompt is advisory, a classifier is probabilistic and beatable by retry, and a bigger model still can't rank trust by origin.

questions.question: In a subagent's Markdown file, what is the body (below the frontmatter)?
options: Its system prompt — it replaces the default assistant for that worker
A description shown in the picker, separate from its actual behavior
A list of example tasks the subagent has completed before
Optional documentation the agent ignores at runtime
questions.explanation: The body becomes the subagent's system prompt; it receives only that (plus basic environment details), not the full default system prompt. It isn't a picker label, a history, or ignored docs.

questions.question: You're defining a code-reviewer subagent. Which tools grant best fits its job?
options: All tools, so it can fix the problems it finds while reviewing
Write and Bash, so it can edit files and commit the fixes itself
Read, Grep, Glob (and run-tests) only — inspect, never write or push
No tools at all — a reviewer only needs the task description, not the code
questions.explanation: Least-privilege: a reviewer needs read + inspect, never write/push — that both improves focus and shrinks the blast radius. Granting write/all tools invites exactly the actions a reviewer shouldn't take; granting none leaves it unable to read the code.

questions.question: Anthropic's orchestrator-worker research system beat a single agent by ~90%. What most explains the gain?
options: The subagents shared one context window, so they never lost information
A larger model was used only in the multi-agent configuration
The multi-agent system skipped verification to answer faster
Specialized subagents ran in parallel with separate contexts and tools
questions.explanation: The gain comes from parallel specialized subagents with separation of concerns (distinct contexts, tools, exploration paths). They do NOT share one context, the lead/worker models were Opus/Sonnet (not a bigger model only in one arm), and nothing was skipped.

questions.question: What does prompt caching actually store and reuse?
options: The model's previous answers, so it can repeat them for free
The processed input prefix, so it doesn't have to be reprocessed
A compressed copy of the whole conversation for later search
Your API key and settings, persisted between sessions
questions.explanation: Caching stores processed input (the prefix), not outputs — so identical-prefix requests skip recomputation. It doesn't store answers, search history, or credentials, and it never changes the model's output.

questions.question: Which choice keeps the cache working across turns?
options: Put the current timestamp in the system prompt so the model knows the time
Reorder the tool definitions each request to surface the relevant one
Freeze the system prompt and tools; append the new question at the end
Switch to a larger model partway through for the harder questions
questions.explanation: The cache is an exact prefix match, so stable content must stay byte-identical and volatile content goes last. Interpolating a timestamp, reordering tools, or switching models all invalidate the prefix.

questions.question: Cache writes cost ~1.25× input and reads ~0.1×. When does caching a prefix LOSE money?
options: When the prefix is read back many times within its TTL
When the prefix is written once and never read again before it expires
Whenever the prompt exceeds the model's minimum cacheable length
Whenever you use the default 5-minute TTL instead of the 1-hour one
questions.explanation: A write costs more than base input, so an entry that's written but never read back is pure overhead. Repeated reads are exactly when caching pays off; exceeding the minimum length and the default TTL are not what cause a loss.
